coredata.dc:title: Rapid hydrogen generation via NaBH4 methanolysis: The synergetic effect of Zn(OH)₂/ZnO@sp2-C heterostructure tuned via biomass-derived carbon nanodots

coredata.dc:description: Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) methanolysis is a promising approach for hydrogen production, however, requires costly noble-metal catalysts and a complicated synthesis route. This study introduces Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx (ZZ@sp2-Cx) nanocomposite as a new catalyst for rapid NaBH4 methanolysis. The catalyst is successfully synthesized via facile one-pot hydrothermal method using biomass-derived carbon nanodots (CNDs) and zinc chloride salt (ZnCl2) as a precursor. It is found that the ratio of CND to deionized water (1:3) is critical for supplying optimal sp2-C in tuning Zn(OH)2/ZnO ratio and excellent catalytic activity of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. ZZ@sp2-C0.05 exhibited notably high catalytic hydrogen generation rate (HGR) of 81.89 L g−1.min−1 within 2.33 min, with low activation energy (Ea) of 14.5 kJ mol−1. Moreover, it maintained 94.6 % catalytic activity after seven consecutive cycles, comparable to noble metals, and outperformed state-of-the-art ZnO-based catalysts. Therefore, utilizing biomass-derived CNDs for tuning Zn(OH)2/ZnO heterostructure for efficient hydrogen generation via NaBH4 methanolysis is promising.

head.ce:title: Rapid hydrogen generation via NaBH4 methanolysis: The synergetic effect of Zn(OH)₂/ZnO@sp2-C heterostructure tuned via biomass-derived carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Science and Technology, Environmental Waste Recycle Institute, Myongji University, Yongin, Gyeonggi-do, 17058, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Science and Technology, Environmental Waste Recycle Institute, Myongji University, Yongin, Gyeonggi-do, 17058, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) methanolysis is a promising approach for hydrogen production, however, requires costly noble-metal catalysts and a complicated synthesis route. This study introduces Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx (ZZ@sp2-Cx) nanocomposite as a new catalyst for rapid NaBH4 methanolysis. The catalyst is successfully synthesized via facile one-pot hydrothermal method using biomass-derived carbon nanodots (CNDs) and zinc chloride salt (ZnCl2) as a precursor. It is found that the ratio of CND to deionized water (1:3) is critical for supplying optimal sp2-C in tuning Zn(OH)2/ZnO ratio and excellent catalytic activity of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. ZZ@sp2-C0.05 exhibited notably high catalytic hydrogen generation rate (HGR) of 81.89 L g−1.min−1 within 2.33 min, with low activation energy (Ea) of 14.5 kJ mol−1. Moreover, it maintained 94.6 % catalytic activity after seven consecutive cycles, comparable to noble metals, and outperformed state-of-the-art ZnO-based catalysts. Therefore, utilizing biomass-derived CNDs for tuning Zn(OH)2/ZnO heterostructure for efficient hydrogen generation via NaBH4 methanolysis is promising.

ce:section.ce:para:
Nowadays, the depletion of non-renewable energy sources coupled with the emission of significant greenhouse gases and global warming shifts scientists and researchers to use renewable energy [1]. Hydrogen (H2) is a renewable and "green" energy source, making the shift to producing green H2 an appealing option for a sustainable energy future [2]. However, the main challenges in utilizing H2 lie in its storage and transportation, for which metal hydrides (MHs) are considered ideal [3]. Amine boranes and MHs are recognized as effective means for storing H2, allowing for on-demand access to the stored energy [3,4]. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) has received great attention due to its high hydrogen content of about 10.9%, cost-effectiveness, non-toxicity, and recyclable nature [5–9]. The H2 production from NaBH4 can be done via hydrolysis (water as a solvent) or alcoholysis (methanol/ethanol as a solvent). The slow kinetics and low conversion of the hydrolysis reaction have led to increased interest in the methanolysis of NaBH4 as an alternative method for producing H2. Moreover, viscous side products such as NaBO2 generated during the NaBH4 hydrolysis are absent in methanolysis only generating NaB(OCH3)4, which can easily dissolve in methanol so that it frees the catalyst surface. Further, NaBH4 methanolysis provides a higher hydrogen generation rate (HGR) compared to NaBH4 hydrolysis [10]. With its lower freezing point, methanol allows hydrogen production at sub-zero temperatures. It supports a closed-loop system where methanol can be regenerated and reused besides its high solubility which enhances the methanolysis process [11,12]. Accordingly, the NaBH4 hydrolysis and methanolysis reactions are represented in equations (1) and (2) as [13]:
(1) NaBH4 + 2H2O → 4H2 + NaBO2
(2) NaBH4 + 4CH3OH → 4H2 + NaB(OCH3)4

ZnO is an extensively studied catalytic material used in applications such as NaBH4 methanolysis, energy harvesting an storage devices [14–16], biomolecular [16], and gas sensors [17]. Its appeal stems from its wide bandgap (3.37 eV), biocompatible, good chemical stability, and outstanding electron mobility [18–21]. For instance, CMS-ZnCl2-CuB demonstrated catalytic activity for NaBH4 methanolysis and achieving a hydrogen generation rate of 17.833 L/min·g cat [22]. Moreover, another study indicates zinc oxide nanoparticles supported on activated carbon (ZnO@AC NPs) showed a hydrogen generation rate of 1.257 L/min·g_cat via NaBH4 methanolysis [23]. Additionally, Mesut B developed an Au/Co/Zn/eggshell catalyst that displayed outstanding catalytic activity, reaching a maximum hydrogen production rate of 4.394 L/min·g cat. However, these hydrogen generation rates are relatively low compared to those observed with metal-based catalysts for NaBH4 methanolysis [9,11,24], suggesting that improvements in catalytic activity is needed. Among, ZnO-based nanocomposites, Zn(OH)2/ZnO heterostructure is a well-known catalyst in electrochemical [25], and photocatalytic applications [25,26]. However, the conductivity, electrochemically active surface area, and morphology of Zn(OH)2/ZnO nanoparticles are tuned by various synthesis routes like heteroatom doping and hybridizing with other metals to induce its catalytic activity [26,27]. For instance, Tashkandi et al. [24] synthesized Ag–ZnO/Zn(OH)2 specimens acquired improved photocatalytic efficacy for nitrobenzene–aniline transformation reaction compared to that of ZnO/Zn(OH)2 specimens. Another study also reported that Ag-decorated ZnO/Zn(OH)2 composite nanoparticles (NPs) were successfully synthesized using the sol-gel technique for photo-degradation of the RhB dyestuff [25].

In our previous study, we designed the optimum ratio of Zn(OH)2/ZnO using a simple hydrothermal method followed by air-annealing for enhanced electrochemical dopamine detection [25]. Summing up, the techniques to obtain Zn(OH)2/ZnO-based heterostructure involve toxic chemicals and complicated synthesis routes, hence the bottleneck is scaling up and replicating. In this regard, green, environmentally benign, and non-toxic synthesis of optimally tuned Zn(OH)2/ZnO heterostructure is needed. Biomass-derived carbon nanodots (CNDs) are used as reducing, phase-directing [28,29], capping, and stabilizing agents for metal and metal oxides, which are used in various applications [30]. CNDs are a new class of carbon nano-allotropes derived from various biomass sources, with particle sizes under 10 nm [31]. Their surfaces feature diverse functional groups, including oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur, which are crucial for their optical fluorescence and allow for straightforward chemical functionalization, providing tunable physical and chemical properties [30,32]. CNDs primarily consist of sp2-carbon (sp2-C), which offers unique features such as chemical tunability, conjugated networks, and the topology of carbon-carbon double bonds. As a result, sp2-C based carbonaceous materials are gaining recognition as exceptional candidates for energy storage and conversion applications [33]. This leads to either a wealth of electrochemically active sites, enhanced conductive networks, or large electrode-electrolyte contact areas, facilitating rapid diffusion and reactions. Consequently, sp2-C rich carbonaceous structures can be effectively modified with functional groups to introduce new functionalities, making them highly valuable for both existing and emerging technologies [33]. Motivated by our previous research [30] where sp2-C tuned morphology and phase of FeOOH/Fe2O3@sp2-C, thereby improving absorption and electrochemical performance towards electrochemical dopamine detection, we extended our investigation if a similar role could be seen in Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx. To the best of our knowledge, no study has been reported on employing CND to tune the ratio of Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx nanocomposite for efficient green hydrogen generation from NaBH4 methanolysis.

In this work, we successfully synthesize Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx nanocomposite using biomass-derived carbon nanodot as-reducing and phased directing agent via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-prepared catalyst showcased excellent catalytic performance for hydrogen generation via NaBH4 methanolysis. Controlling the ratio of CND to DI water is crucial in forming Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx nanocomposite optimum being 1:3. It is evident that the sp2-C has played a key role in fine-tuning the ratio of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO in Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. We also found that the synergetic effect of sp2-C and the optimized ratio of Zn(OH)2/ZnO possess superior catalytic activity of hydrogen generation from NaBH4 methanolysis. Various analytical techniques are employed to investigate Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx nanocomposites such as TGA, UV–vis, XRD, FT-IR, FE-SEM, and XPS. This study highlights that biomass-derived CNDs are crucial for adjusting and optimizing Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx towards enhanced NaBH4 methanolysis in search of green hydrogen toward a sustainable future.

Pumpkin skin was prepared by peeling off pumpkin which was obtained from Yongin market (Yongin, South Korea) and dried at room temperature before it was ground to obtain pumpkin skin (PS) powder. Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9%), Zinc chloride (96.0%), Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99%), and methanol (CH3OH,99.9%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Korea). Meanwhile, ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 98 %) and urea chemical (99–100.5%) were purchased from Showa Chemicals (Japan). All chemicals were used without further treatment. The deionized water (DI) 's electrical resistance is 18.2 MΩ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pumpkin skin carbon nanodot
ce:section.ce:para: Pumpkin skin carbon nanodot (PS-CND) was synthesized according to our previous method [30]. Briefly, a known mass of pumpkin skin (PS) powder was mixed with 100 ml of deionized water and subjected to sonication for over 5 min for better dispersion. The resulting solution was then transferred into a sealed 150 ml Teflon-Lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 12 h in a hydrothermal oven. After natural cooling at room temperature, the brownish liquid, identified as carbon nanodot (CND) was separated from the solid residue (hydro char) via centrifugation. This process led to the creation of CND.

The synthesis of Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx (ZZ@sp2-Cx) heterostructure was carried out by modifying our previous method [30]. Detail procedure is as follows: in a hydrothermal vessel, 0.75 g ZnCl2, 1 g NH4F, and 0.8 g urea were added to a mixture of 25 ml of CND and 75 ml of DI (maintaining a 1 to 3 ratio) then ultrasonicated for over 5 h to improve dispersion. The vessel was then tightly sealed and subjected to heat at 120 °C in an oven for 12 h. Following natural cooling, the light-black precipitate obtained was thoroughly washed with DI and ethanol until a pH = 7.0 was achieved. Subsequently, the washed precipitate was then dried in a vacuum oven for 8 h. Our previous study found that CND consists of various carbon structures, with sp2-C being the primary and most substantial component, accounting for approximately 0.02 g sp2-C in every 10 mL of CND utilized [30]. Therefore, 25 mL of CND contains 0.05g of sp2-C, thus the resulting nanocomposite was denoted as ZZ@sp2-C0.05 for ease of recalling, as depicted in Scheme 1 . A similar procedure was followed to obtain ZZ@sp2-Cx with different ratios of CND to DI such as (1–9), (1–1), and (1–0) donated as ZZ@sp2-C0.02, ZZ@sp2-C0.1, and ZZ@sp2-C0.2, respectively. Additionally, Zn(OH)2/ZnO was prepared using a similar synthesis approach without CND addition as a control sample and denoted as C-ZZ.

Hydrogen generation via NaBH4 methanolysis was conducted on ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite using the traditional water displacement method as reported in previous studies [3]. A reaction was conducted in a jacketed glass reactor attached to a lab companion chiller unit (South Korea) to maintain the reaction temperature and the reaction procedure was elaborated as follows: briefly, in a typical reaction, 10 mg of ZZ@sp2-Cx catalyst was added to a glass jacketed glass reactor containing 20 mL methanol with stirring at 750 rpm at 1 atm until uniform dispersion was achieved followed by the addition of 1.25 M of NaBH4. The volume of hydrogen generated was directly recorded by measuring the displaced water volume from the conical flask into the accumulator placed on the measuring balance and automatically displayed on the computer with the ’balance talk” software as shown in Scheme 2 . Volume of H2 (mL) was automatically recorded every 10 s to determine the hydrogen generation rate (HGR). Consequently, HGR was calculated using Equation (3) [4]. It is important to mention that the NaBH4 reaction was replicated at least three times to curb experimental errors and average values were used. Moreover, the catalytic performance of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 was investigated under different operating conditions such as catalyst loading and NaBH4 concentration varied from 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, and 15 mg, and 0.5, 1, 1.25, and 1.5 M, respectively. The kinetics of the NaBH4 methanolysis on ZZ@sp2-C0.05 was studied at varied reaction temperatures (15, 25, 35, and 45 °C).
(3) HGR=V
H2 /(t × m
w )
where t is the reaction time (min), and m
w is the weight of the catalyst (g).

Morphological characterization of the synthesized nanomaterials was conducted using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (S-2400, Hitachi). To determine the chemical state of the nanomaterials, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) was performed using VG Scientific ESCALAB 250. Moreover, surface functionalities were analyzed through Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (NICOLET iS5, Thermo Scientific). The crystal structure was examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a D8 ADVANCE. Additionally, thermal stability was investigated using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using a SCINCO TGA N-1000 analyzer. Furthermore, the specific surface area, pore size, and porosity of Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-C were investigated through the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, based on N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms obtained at −196 °C using a BELSORP-Max (BEL Japan Inc).

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were successfully prepared from PS using a straightforward hydrothermal method following the same procedure as our previous study [30]. Building our basis on our previous finding where the CND functioned as a reducing, structural, and phase-directing agent for FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite for enhanced dopamine detection [30], in this study, we extended further our investigation of whether CND influences the structure of other transition metals, for example, Zn2+ to form Zn(OH)2/ZnO heterostructure. Hence, we first investigate crystal structure using X-ray diffraction analysis.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern revealed that the sp2-C content in the nanocomposite affects the crystal structure of ZZ@sp2-C. Moreover, the XRD patterns of C-ZZ nanocomposite are depicted in Fig. 1 A. Consequently, diffraction peaks of C-ZZ nanocomposite at 2θ value of 20° correspond to orthorhombic Zn(OH)2 (with JCPDS# 00-024-1444) and diffraction peaks at 31.2°, 33.4°, 36.3°, 47.1°, 56.1°, and 62.1° were assigned to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110) and (103) planes of hexagonal wurtzite Zinc oxide (with JCPDS# 00-036-1451), respectively, revealing the existence of Zn(OH)2 and ZnO in C-ZZ nanocomposite aligning to the previous studies [34]. Remarkably, ZZ@sp2-Cx (Fig. 1B–E) nanocomposite maintained a similar structural feature of C-ZZ crystal except with a marked decrease in the intensity of at a 2θ value of 20° compared to the peak intensity of at 2θ value of 35°. Surprisingly no diffraction peaks for CND are observed in the ZZ@sp2-C0.02 (Fig. 1B), ZZ@sp2-C0.05 (Fig. 1C), and ZZ@sp2-C0.1 (Fig. 1D) nanocomposites for sp2-C content of 0.02, 0.05, and 0.1g respectively, due to the weak crystallization nature of the CND or low carbon content similar to the previous study [30]. Notably, the intensity of CND diffraction peaks increased with sp2-C content, particularly at 0.2 g in ZZ@sp2-C0.2 (Fig. 1E), confirming the successful synthesis of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. Moreover, as the sp2-C content increases the peak intensity at the 2θ value of 20° corresponding to Zn(OH)2 is decreasing; however, the peak intensity for ZnO becomes more prominent. For instance, the peak intensity ratio of Zn(OH)2 in (100) indices located at 2θ value of 20° to the prominent ZnO in (101) indices located at 2θ value of 35.5° was determined to be 1.45, 1.279, 1.13, 0.9 and 0.81 for C-ZZ, ZZ@sp2-c0.02, ZZ@sp2-c0.05, ZZ@sp2-C0.1, and ZZ@sp2-C0.2, respectively, shown in Table S4. This highlights the sp2-C′s enhancement for the conversion of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO in the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. Moreover, this further suggests the influence of sp2-C in fine-tuning the Zn(OH)2/ZnO in ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite.

Furthermore, the crystalline size of nanocomposite is determined from interplanar spacing (d-spacing) and lattice parameters, using Debye Scherrer's and Bragg's law equation (1) by applying the methodology highlighted elsewhere [35].
(4)
D (hkl )=(Kλ)/(β hkl) × Cos θ (hkl)
where D is the particle size in nm, β hkl is FWHM is full width at half maximum dominant peak intensity, K is a Scherrer's constant (0.94) λ is the wavelength of X-ray source (1.54 Å for CuKA radiation), and θ is a peak position. The (101) plane was chosen to calculate the crystallite size in as-prepared ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite which is determined to be 3.41 Å, 3.765 Å, 4.234 Å, 4.239 Å and 8.469 Å for C-ZZ, ZZ@sp2-C0.02, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, ZZ@sp2-C0.1, and ZZ@sp2-C0.2, respectively. Furthermore, we conducted TGA, FTIR, UV–vis, and XPS analysis if the sp2-C affects the phase of nanocomposite as it did for crystal structure as shown in XRD. This will be explained later.

Moreover, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed to quantify sp2-C content in the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. Accordingly, Fig. 2 A demonstrates weight loss percentage of 12.01 %, 13.4 %, 17.39 %, and 18.69 % corresponding to ZZ@sp2-C0.02, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, Z@sp2-C0.1, and ZZ@sp2-C0.2 respectively. This weight loss is attributed to CND degradation [30,36,37]increased with sp2-C content, with significant weight losses observed between 300 and 500 °C. It is revealed that the presence of CND in the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite is proportional to the noticeable increase of sp2-C content in the composite, which confirms with XRD analysis. Additionally, this is evidenced by a color change on the synthesized ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite as displayed in Fig. S1. Hence the color of synthesized ZZ@sp2-C0.02 (Fig. S2B), and other ZZ@sp2-Cx (Fig. S1 C to E) nanocomposite closely resembles light brown and light black, respectively, which is quite different from white color of C-ZZ (Fig. S1A). This observation further highlights that as a further increase in the sp2-C loading the color of the nanocomposite turned from white to light black, reviling the synthesis of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) investigation was conducted to assess the functional group found in the as-prepared nanocomposites such as C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx, shown in Fig. 2B. The absorption peaks at 660 and 923 cm−1 wavenumbers were assigned to the stretching vibrations of Zn–OH [38,39], whereas, the peak at 750 cm−1 corresponds to Zn–O vibrations in the ZnO matrix [40] revealing the existence of Zn(OH)2 and ZnO in the ZZ@sp2-CX nanocomposite, respectively. Additionally, the peak observed at wavenumbers 1430 and 1630 cm−1 could be attributed to the deformation of the vibrational mode of Zn–OH and hydroxyl groups (O–H), respectively [37]. The characteristic band between 3800 and 3000 cm−1 could be due to the vibration mode of H–O–H, implicating water molecules absorbed on the surface of C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx, respectively. Moreover, the peak intensity of wavelength located at 660 and 923 cm−1 of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite was decreased as the sp2-C loading increased compared to C-ZZ which further evidenced the transformation of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO. Furthermore, in contrast to the C-ZZ nanocomposite, the peak shift was exhibited at 930 cm−1 attributed to Zn(OH)2 in the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. This finding suggests the successful incorporation of sp2-C in the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite aligning with the discussions highlighted from the XRD and TGA analysis.

The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx samples are depicted in Fig. 2C. It is noticed that C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx samples absorb in the visible region. Moreover, the absorbance edge in ZZ@sp2-C is shifted towards a higher wavelength which could be associated with the conversion of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO due to the inclusion of sp2-C in the as-prepared nanocomposite [26]. In addition, the extent to which the absorption edge is shifted depends on the mass of sp2-C loading. Moreover, bandgap energy is determined using the Kubelka-Munk equation, for direct bandgap determination given by Equation (5) as [41,42]:
(5)
αhv = A(hv-E
g )0.5
where E
g is photon energy, h is Planck's constant (6.6 × 10−34J.s), v is the frequency of the electromagnetic radiation, A is a proportionality constant, and α is the absorption coefficient [41,42]. The band gap energy values can be determined from the Tauc plot of (ahv)2 versus the photon energy (hv) curve (Fig. 2D) which is found to be 2.5, 2.45, 2.33, 2.28, and 2.23 eV for C-ZZ, ZZ@sp2-C0.01, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, ZZ@sp2-C0.1 and ZZ@sp2-C0.2, respectively. The bandgap energy shifted down as the sp2-C content increased in the ZZ@sp2-C nanocomposite compared to C-ZZ. This suggests that the conversion of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO aligns with the XRD and FTIR analysis. This was indicative of sp2-C in the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite facilitates the conversion of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO. The lower bandgap energy of the ZZ@sp2-Cx is good evidence for greater catalytic activity of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite compared to C-ZZ nanocomposite agreeing with the previous literature [27].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was employed to investigate the surface chemical composition and electronic state in the C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. Fig. S2A shows the binding energy at 284.6 eV, 531.5 eV, and 1021.16 eV was attributed to C 1s, O 1s, and Zn 2p spectra, respectively, revealing ZZ@sp2-Cx is mainly composed of Zn, O, and C elements. Fig. S2B shows XPS spectra of Zn 2p in C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposites displaying two typical peaks at a binding energy of 1021.7 and 1044.8 eV assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. The spin-orbit splitting of 23.1 eV, indicating that Zn exhibited at +2 valance state in the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite [43]. Moreover, the core XPS spectra for Zn 2p were zoomed corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 spectra in C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx showing two deconvoluted peaks at binding energies approximately 1020.5 eV and 1021.58 Ev, which attributes to Zn–O of ZnO and Zn–OH of Zn(OH)2, (Figs. 3(A-1) to (D-1), respectively [44–46]. This finding reveals, the existence of ZnO and Zn(OH)2 in C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite. The integrated area ratio of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO decreased with increased sp2-C, confirming the transition from Zn(OH)2 to ZnO, in alignment with previous analyses as shown in Table S1. Moreover, the ratio of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO in the ZZ@sp2-C0.05 nanocomposite is 1.01 approaches unity, suggesting a uniform distribution of both components. Interestingly, this finding aligns with the XRD analysis, where the peak intensity ratio of Zn(OH)2 at (110) to ZnO at (101) is about 1.13 approximately close to unity.

Figs. 3(A-2) to (D-2) shows the O 1s spectra of C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx were deconvoluted into two at binding energies approximately 530.7 eV attributed to lattice oxygen of Zn–O and 531 eV which is associated with adsorbed oxygen (Zn–OH) bonding [47]. This ascertains the existence of Zn(OH)2 and ZnO in the as-prepared nanocomposites [36]. Table S1 presents the summary of the relative ratio of the hydroxy group (Zn–OH) to lattice oxygen (Zn–O) was determined by integrating the area under the curve. Accordingly, 2.28, 1.75, 0.99, and 0.87 were determined in C-ZZ, ZZ@sp2-C0.02, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, and ZZ@sp2-C0.1, respectively. From the O 1s and Zn 2p3/2 result, as the sp2-C in the nanocomposite increased the ratio of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO under the area of the curve decreased suggesting the transformation of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO, which supports the finding of XRD, FTIR, and UV–vis analysis. Moreover, Fig. 3 (A-3) to (D-3) shows C 1s spectra of ZZ@sp2-Cx deconvoluted to three peaks around 284.2 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.8 eV attributed to sp2-C, sp3-C, and COOH, respectively [48–51]. However, the intensity of sp2-C in ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite is more prominent than sp3-C which suggests the dominance of sp2-C over the sp3-C in the ZZ@sp2-C nanocomposite. Furthermore, the C1s spectra of C-ZZ were further deconvoluted into two peaks at a binding energy of 286.9 eV and 288.5 eV attributed to -C-O-C/-OH and –CO, respectively. This suggests that the sp2-C in ZZ@sp2-Cx is from the CND. We also performed SEM and TEM analysis to determine whether sp2-C affects the morphology of ZZ@sp2-Cx.

FE-SEM images presented in Fig. 4 A correspond to C-ZZ revealing that the nanomaterial is composed of a hexagonal morphology. However, after the incorporation of sp2-C from CND (Fig. 4B), the morphology of the ZZ@sp2-C0.05 (Fig. 4C), and ZZ@sp2-Cx variants was altered to microspheres composite of nanosheet, where the Zn(OH)2/ZnO nanosheets are modified on the CND surface, shown in Fig. S3. Additionally, TEM image on ZZ@sp2-C0.05 nanocomposite variants revealed the growth of Zn nanosheets on the surface of sp2-C in which the nanosheets are composed of overlapped and translucent layers confirming the fine-tuning effect of sp2-C as shown in Fig. 4E. A closer look at HR-TEM images of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 and ZZ@sp2-Cx shows two distinct fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.251 nm and 0.427 nm corresponding to the ZnO (101) and Zn(OH)2 (100) XRD planes (Fig. 4F and Fig. S3), respectively. This suggests the presence of Zn(OH)2 and ZnO in ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite which supports XRD, FTIR, and XPS analysis. The EDS analysis of the C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-C demonstrates the uniform Zn, O, and C distribution in the as-prepared nanocomposite (Fig. 4G–I and Fig. S4). Additionally, as the sp2-C loading increases, the percentage of oxygen in the prepared nanocomposite decreases while the percentage of carbon rises as shown in Table S2. The reduction in the percentage of oxygen could be associated with the conversion of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO in ZZ@sp2-Cx due to the incorporation of sp2-C.

The microstructure of as-prepared C-ZZ and ZZ@sp2-Cx variants was investigated on Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) using N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, shown in Fig. S3. It was observed that the catalyst had a type III isotherm and was a typical H3 hysteresis loop confirming the presence of microporous and mesoporous microstructure in the P/Po range of 0.9–1 [52]. The BET surface area for C-ZZ, ZZ@sp2-C0.02, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, ZZ@sp2-C0.1, and ZZ@sp2-C0.2 nanocomposite was determined as 0.53, 6.191, 18.9, 17.1, and 8.9 m2/g, respectively. Interestingly, the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposites exhibit a higher specific surface area compared to C-ZZ revealing that sp2-C enhances the specific surface area of the ZZ@sp2-Cx. The average pore diameter and average pore volume distribution were obtained from the BJH plot, as illustrated in the insert of Fig. S5 and Table 1 , respectively. BET also reveals that the incorporation of sp2-C affects the pore structure of the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the catalytic performance via NaBH4 methanolysis
H2 generation performance of as-prepared nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The H2 generation performance of the as-prepared nanocomposite via NaBH4 methanolysis was investigated in the presence of 20 mL of methanol and 1.25 M NaBH4 solution in 10 mg of C-ZZ, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, sp2-C, and Com. ZnO nanomaterial, respectively. The catalytic conversion of NaBH4 methanolysis takes approximately 2.33, 11.5, 12.6, 15.5, and 21 min for ZZ@sp2-C0.05, sp2-C, Com. ZnO, C-ZZ, and self-methanolysis as shown in Fig. 5 A. The corresponding hydrogen generation rate (HGR) was determined by utilizing Equation (3) after the complete release of H2 stored. A remarkable HGR exhibited by ZZ@sp2-C0.05 is 81.895 L/g.min at 2.33 min which is 1.6x, 2.31x, 2.34x, and 4.5x higher compared to sp2-C (52.188 L/g.min), Com. ZnO (35.39 L/g.min), C-ZZ (34.895 L/g.min), and self-methanolysis (18.159 L/g.min), respectively, (Fig. 5B). As observed from the HGR, sp2-C also demonstrates significant activity compared to com. ZnO, C-ZZ, and self-methanolysis. This indicates that sp2-C functions not only as a composite tuning agent but also as an active catalyst for H2 generation on its own. This could be due to the presence of functional groups on the CND surface such as carboxylics, phenolics, lactonics, and etheric groups that might influence the acid/base and redox properties of carbon [22]. Moreover, the faster hydrogen generation rate of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 attributed to strong interactions between Zn2+ ions of ZnCl2 salt with hydroxyl and amine of CND are likely to occur which is also used as a Lewis acid that catalyzes the heterolytic chemical reactions of ZnCl2, C–O, and C–C groups [22]. Taken together, the excellent catalytic activity of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 toward hydrogen generation from methanolysis of NaBH4 is attributed to the synergistic effect between sp2-C and Zn(OH)2/ZnO. Therefore, the ZZ@sp2-C0.05 catalyst was utilized in further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of sp2-C loading on the catalytic activity of Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-C
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of varying sp2-C loading on the catalytic performance of ZZ@sp2-Cx for NaBH4 methanolysis was studied and displayed in Fig. 5C. Accordingly, the H2 study on ZZ@sp2-Cx variants such as ZZ@sp2-C0.02, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, ZZ@sp2-C0.1, and ZZ@sp2-C0.2 nanocomposite was carried out at 25 °C in the presence of 20 mL of methanol and 1.25 M NaBH4. As a result, it took 10.5, 2.33, 10, and 12.6 min for complete release of H2 stored using ZZ@sp2-C0.02, ZZ@sp2-C0.05, ZZ@sp2-C0.1, and ZZ@sp2-C0.2 nanocomposites, respectively. Notably, ZZ@sp2-C0.05 demonstrated superior hydrogen generation performance in NaBH4 methanolysis compared to control samples, achieving saturation at 2.33 min and producing 1915.7 mL volume of H2 which is 2.04x, 1.69x, and 1.96x higher compared ZZ@sp2-C0.02 (931.55 mL), ZZ@sp2-C0.1 (1128.3 mL), ZZ@sp2-C0.2 (972.35 mL), respectively. The maximum HGR obtained was by ZZ@sp2-C0.05, which is 81.895 L/g.min at 2.33min which is 2.04x, 1.69x, and 1.96x higher compared to ZZ@sp2-C0.02 (39.980 L/g.min), ZZ@sp2-C0.1 (48.424 L/g.min), ZZ@sp2-C0.2 (41.7317 L/g.min) (Fig. 5D). It has been noticed that there is a reduction in HGR during NaBH4 methanolysis, particularly, at lower (0.02 g) and higher (0.2 g) sp2-C loading compared to 0.05g sp2-C. This can be attributed to two main factors: (i) at lower sp2-C levels, the production of ZnO is limited, leading to insufficient ZnO, while Zn(OH)2 becomes dominant, which diminishes the role of ZnO in catalytic activity; and (ii) as sp2-C loading increases, there may be an excess of ZnO, which reduces the influence of Zn(OH)2 on catalytic performance. Therefore, optimum sp2-C loading is needed to generate the optimum ratio of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO, i.e., 0.05 g sp2-C as confirmed from XRD and XPS analysis.

Moreover, it is evident from Fig. 5A that sp2-C exhibits a decent hydrogen generation rate on its own, indicating its inherent activity for hydrogen generation. Further, sp2-C in the nanocomposite (ZZ@sp2-Cx) improved the catalytic performance of C-ZZ by yielding a better hydrogen generation performance compared to bare C-ZZ (without sp2-C). Moreover, the TGA investigation (Fig. 2A) insights that at lower sp2-C mass loading, i.e., ZZ@sp2-C0.02 (39.980 L/g.min) and higher sp2-C mass loading, i.e., ZZ@sp2-C0.2 (41.731 L/g.min), the hydrogen generation rate at saturation is neck-to-neck, which is only 1.043X higher at a higher sp2-C loading, despite sp2-C mass loading ratio is 10X higher. This highlights the quantities of sp2-C may not be majorly influencing the catalytic performance of ZZ@sp2-C nanocomposite but the Zn(OH)2/ZnO could. This is because ZZ@sp2-C0.02 which contains excess Zn(OH)2 followed by ZZ@sp2-C0.1 and ZZ@sp2-C0.2, as detailed from the XRD and XPS investigations, demonstrate lower catalytic activity for hydrogen generation compared to ZZ@sp2-C0.05 which has an optimum ratio of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO. This indicates the outstanding catalytic activity of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 may not be solely attributed to the incorporation of sp2-C in ZZ@sp2-Cx via optimal Zn(OH)2 to ZnO ratio. In other words, the excellent catalytic performance of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 via NaBH4 methanolysis for hydrogen generation is due to the synergetic effect resulting from the optimal ratio of Zn(OH)2 to ZnO by sp2-C incorporation.

In terms of the morphology of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposites, all variants displayed a nanosheet structure, and they all exhibited faster HGR compared to the C-ZZ hexagonal structure. Hence morphology is not the major factor contributing to the excellent HGR performance of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 despite it having a contributing effect. However, the microstructure nature of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 shows a specific surface area of 18.9 m2/g. This value is 34.5X, 3.05X, 1.1X, and 2.1X higher compared to C-ZZ(0.56 m2/g), ZZ@sp2-C0.02 (6.191 m2/g), ZZ@sp2-C0.1 (17.1 m2/g), and ZZ@sp2-C0.2 (8.96 m2/g), respectively, as illustrated in Table 2 . Notably, the specific surface area ratio of the optimal material (ZZ@sp2-C0.05) to C-ZZ is approximately 34.5, more than 10 folds higher than their corresponding ratio of the hydrogen generation rate, which is only 2.34. This discrepancy indicates that the specific surface area is not the major limiting factor in hydrogen generation via NaBH4 methanolysis. Moreover, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the as-prepared nanocomposite is determined from the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of non-faradic region using cyclic voltammetry (0–0.35V) in the presence of a 7.0 pH of 0.5 M buffer (PBS) analyte as shown in Fig. S6. The ECSA of the nanocomposite is obtained from the slope of the linear graph of (Ia-Ic)/2 versus the scan rate [53] as shown in Fig. 6 . Accordingly, the ECSA of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 is 4.17E-7 F cm−2, which is 2.23X, 2.06X, 1.7X, and 2.0X higher than C-ZZ (1.87E-07 F cm−2), ZZ@sp2-C0.02 (2.05E-07 F cm−2), ZZ@sp2-C0.1 (2.36E-07 F cm−2), ZZ@sp2-C0.2 (2.06E-07 F cm−2), respectively (Table S3). Notably, ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposite, particularly, with sp2-C loading of 0.05g (ZZ@sp2-C0.05) possesses larger ECSA compared to C-ZZ nanocomposite and control samples suggesting the incorporation of sp2-C in the nanocomposite enhances the electrochemically active surface area of the nanocomposite. Interestingly, HGR and ECSA follow a similar trend for hydrogen generation performance see section 3.2.2 . The higher ECSA of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 provides accessible sites for quick adsorption of reactants, i.e., methanol/NaBH4 on the surface of the catalyst, according to Langmuir-Hinshelwood adsorption-desorption model similar to previous findings [4,54,55]. Thus, higher ECSA of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 supplies sufficient active sites and augments the contact between the catalyst-reactant interface thereby enhancing the hydrogen generation rate via NaBH4 methanolysis [56]. This was further proved by the activity of the catalysts for fuel cells increased with rising their electrochemically active surface area [9,53]. Therefore, the ECSA could be a major factor for ZZ@sp2-C0.05's superior electrocatalytic activity toward NaBH4 methanolysis. The faster HGR was exhibited for the sp2-C loading of 0.05g, therefore the succeeding experiments were performed with ZZ@sp2-C0.05.

Regarding the NaBH4 methanolysis mechanism, the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model was adopted on ZZ@sp2-Cx. Initially, NaBH4 and CH3OH were adsorbed on the ZZ@sp2-Cx surface and dissociates where BH4 − favors Lewis acidic site (Zn(OH)2 δ+) to form H δ− and BH3 − while CH3OH likes a Lewis basic site (ZnO δ−) to form H δ+ and OCH3 − species, respectively. Meanwhile, ZnO δ− coordinates with methanol and facilitates the activation of methanol through protonation. The hydroxyl groups from Zn(OH)2 can enhance the nucleophilicity of BH4 − [59]. This makes the O–H bond in methanol more reactive to generate H δ+. The activated methanol can interact with the BH4 −, leading to the formation of H2 via coupling of H δ− and H δ+ and the intermediate BH3OCH3 − species [60]. Consequently, BH3 (OCH3)- on the catalyst surface reacts with CH3OH in the reaction mixture generating another H2 leaving BH2(CH3O)2 - species. This procedure exceeds until all the hydrogen stored in BH4 − is released, i.e., 4H2 as the detailed mechanism is displayed in Scheme 3 (as shown below). Note that the total H2 released during NaBH4 methanolysis is half from NaBH4 and the remaining half is from CH3OH, according to reference [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst loading on HGR
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of catalyst loading on H2 generation via NaBH4 methanolysis was performed in the presence of 20 mL methanol, 25 °C, and 1.25 M NaBH4 within catalyst loading of (5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, and 15 mg) as shown in Fig. 7 A. The completion times of NaBH4 methanolysis with 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, and 15 mg catalyst loading were found to be 13, 5.83, 2.33, 9.5, and 11.6 min, respectively. The volume of H2 generated at 10 mg loading was 1915.7 mL (81.895 L/g.min) saturated in just 2.33 min which is 1.3X, 1.04X, 2.5X, and 3.17X higher compared to 5 mg (62.746 L/g.min), 7.5 mg (80.032 L/g.min), 12.5 mg (32.515 L/g.min), and 15 mg (25.753 L/g.min), respectively. It can be observed that the hydrogen generation rate increases from 62.746 L/g.min to 81.895 L/g.min as the catalyst loading increases from 5 to 10 mg, respectively, and decreases to 25.753 L/g.min at increasing catalyst loading to 15 mg (Fig. 7B). Suggesting that the hydrogen generation is influenced by catalyst concentration. At very low catalyst loading hydrogen generation performance was low due to the scarcity of active sites. On the other hand, at high catalyst loading ZZ@sp2-C0.05 is involved in a reaction where excess amounts of the active site are competing for the same amount of NaBH4 concentration, as a result, the hydrogen generation rate declines [22,62]. Hence the optimum catalyst loading is 10 mg. Therefore, 10 mg of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 was utilized throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NaBH4 concentration on HGR
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of NaBH4 concentration on hydrogen generation was studied in the presence of 20 mL of methanol, and 10 mg of catalyst at 25 °C, using varying NaBH4 concentrations of 0.5, 1, 1.25, and 1.5 M as shown in Fig. 7C. The NaBH4 methanolysis rate increases linearly with the increase in NaBH4 concentration and then declined at higher NaBH4 concentrations. Accordingly, the HGR from the NaBH4 methanolysis with 0.5, 1, 125, and 1.5 M were found to be 13.377, 21.008, 81.895, and 39.980 L/g.min, respectively (Fig. 7D). The maximum HGR obtained being 81.895 L g−1.min−1 at 1.25 M NaBH4 loading. Notably, with an increase in the concentration of NaBH4, HGR increases via NaBH4 methanolysis. However, there is a significant decline in HGR by increasing the amount of NaBH4 from 1.25 to 1.5 M. This reduction could be attributed to the fixed amount of catalyst which has limited active sites. As the NaBH4 concentration increases, the active site of the catalyst is gradually occupied by BH4 − and their number riches saturation [63]. Further, an increase in NaBH4 concentration may increase the viscosity of the solution, leading to a slower mass transfer from NaBH4 to the catalyst surface and eventually ending up in lower HGR [57]. Thus, the optimum NaBH4 concentration of 1.25 M was utilized in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature on HGR
ce:section.ce:para:
The influence of temperature on H2 generation from NaBH4 methanolysis was investigated at varied temperatures of 15, 25, 35, and 45 °C by maintaining 10 mg of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 and 1.25 M NaBH4 solution in 20 mL of methanol constant. Fig. 7E illustrates the volume of H2 generated over time. It was observed from the result the rate of H2 generation is accelerated with increasing the reaction temperature Fig. 7F. Hence, the rate of hydrogen generation increased proportionally with an increase in temperature. Therefore, it is evident that H2 generation is completed in a shorter time as the temperature increases. The activation energy (Ea) for the NaBH4 methanolysis reaction was determined from the Arrhenius and Ering plots generated from Equation (7) after taking the natural logarithm of Equation (6), respectively [64].
(6) K = A exp(-Ea/RT)
(7) ln (K
obs ) = ln(A) – (Ea/R)(1/T)
where A is a constant known as the Arrhenius factor, K
obs is the observed reaction rate constant at the reaction rate constant at the reaction temperature T, Ea is the activation energy, and R is the universal gas constant (8. 314 J. k−1.mol−1). The activation energy (Ea) for the reaction catalyzed by ZZ@sp2-C0.05 was determined from the slope of the Arrhenius plot to be 14.5 kJ/mol, as illustrated in Fig. 8 C. Notably, this value is lower compared with those catalysts synthesized in recent literature [7,11,24,57,58]. The result shows that Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-C0.05 has a low Ea of 14.5 kJ/mol comparable to state-of-the-art ZnO-based nanomaterials (Table 2). This indicates its excellent catalytic performance in the methanolysis of NaBH4, with the ability to complete the reaction even at a low temperature of 15 °C. Therefore, it has the potential to be used in hydrogen power systems as a non-toxic, reliable, and environmentally friendly catalyst for NaBH4 methanolysis.

The reusability and recyclability of a catalytic material is crucial for scale-up and commercial applications [65]. Accordingly, the recyclability test of the ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposites in NaBH4 methanolysis was performed by measuring the volume of H2 produced as a function of time for seven consecutive cycles. To this end, the 10 mg of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 was utilized by maintaining NaBH4 concentration, and reaction temperature at 1.25 M NaBH4, and 25 °C constant in 20 mL of methanol solution. After every cycle, ZZ@sp2-C0.05 was recycled via washing with the ultrapure DI water before being kept in the oven drying at 90 °C for 12 h. The H2 generation rate (HGR) in the first cycle reached 81.895 L g−1.min⁻1. In the second cycle, the HGR was 79.453 L min⁻1 until it reached an HGR of 77.485 L g−1.min−1 in the seventh cycle, maintaining 94.6 % HGR compared to the pristine first cycle. This demonstrates the catalyst's sustained and durable activity over seven consecutive reactions in NaBH4 methanolysis without significant decay, indicating good stability and reusability (refer to Fig. 8A and B). However, the reaction rate decreased approximately by only 6% compared to the rate of the fresh catalyst after the seventh reuse by normalizing the volume of hydrogen generated to catalyst weight after every run [4]. This slight decrease in catalytic activity observed after the seventh reuse of ZZ@sp2-C0.05 for NaBH4 methanolysis might be attributed to the loss of sufficient catalytically active sites due to the loss of catalyst material during the washing and recycling processes [22]. Moreover, the regeneration of ZZ@sp2-Cx as shown in Fig. 8C, was carried out using a previously reported method from Ref. [66]. In brief, we washed ZZ@sp2-Cx with 20 mL of 1 M HCl solution after the seventh run, and then employed it for NaBH4 methanolysis under conditions similar to those of the pristine ZZ@sp2-Cx, despite a loss of catalyst weight during handling steps such as washing, centrifuging, and drying. The results from the catalyst regeneration test demonstrated that ZZ@sp2-Cx was effectively regenerated, retaining 99.8% of its initial performance when normalized by catalyst weight. Therefore, our proposed catalyst remains effective even after the seventh regeneration cycle.

In this study, the optimum ratio of Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-Cx (ZZ@sp2-Cx) nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by utilizing CND as a green reducing and phase-directing agent via one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-prepared catalyst was utilized for hydrogen generation from NaBH4 methanolysis. The sp2-C in the CND loading is varied from 0.02 to 0.2g to optimize the Zn(OH)2/ZnO heterostructure. The synthesis and optimization of ZZ@sp2-Cx nanocomposites were confirmed by XRD, FTIR, UV, TGA, SEM, TEM, HR-TEM, and XPS analysis. The effect of sp2-C loading, reaction temperature, NaBH4 concentration, and catalyst loading on the hydrogen generation rate was investigated. Thus, Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-C0.05 (ZZ@sp2-C0.05) nanocomposite (sp2-C loading of 0.05 g) demonstrated excellent catalytic performance for NaBH4 methanolysis for hydrogen generation with a hydrogen generation rate (HGR) of 81.895 L g−1.min−1 at a reaction completion time of 2.33 min. Moreover, ZZ@sp2-C0.05 exhibited a low activated energy (Ea) value of 14.5 kJ mol−1 which is lower than the performance of numerous state-of-the-art ZnO-based catalysts [7,11,24,57,58]. Furthermore, ZZ@sp2-C0.05 retains its 94.6 % performance compared to its pristine after the seventh run. Therefore, using biomass-derived CNDs to synthesize Zn(OH)2/ZnO@sp2-C nanocomposites for H2 generation through NaBH₄ methanolysis has a promising future.
